# Guidance — DEL-019-04: Construction Quality Control

**Document type:** Guidance (directional)
**Deliverable ID:** DEL-019-04_QC-Management
**Generated by:** 4_DOCUMENTS Pass 1+2; enriched Pass 3
**Date:** 2026-02-26

---

## Purpose

DEL-019-04 exists to give the Proponent (Design-Builder) a structured, documented quality control program that governs the construction of the WDMLRL Maintenance Shop Addition from groundbreaking through to Construction Completion Certificate (CCC) issuance.

The deliverable serves two overlapping functions:

1. **Contractual compliance:** The RFP explicitly requires the Proponent to describe and be accountable for quality control — including construction oversight, material quality control, reporting, and correction of deficient work (RFP §4.7.5). This was a scored evaluation criterion (5% weighting, RFP §5.1.2 Item G). A documented QC program is the execution artifact that fulfils this commitment.

2. **Objective alignment:** DEL-019-04 directly supports OBJ-001 (deliver a code-compliant, fully functional maintenance shop that passes all applicable inspections) and OBJ-007 (maintain safe site operations as Prime Contractor with zero lost-time incidents). Without systematic quality control, neither objective is reliably achievable.

(Source: decomposition OBJ-001, OBJ-007; RFP §4.7.5, §5.1.2; _CONTEXT.md — Linked Objectives)

---

## Principles

### P1 — Prevention over detection

The QC program should be structured to identify quality issues before they are embedded in completed work. Inspection hold points and material verification before placement are more cost-effective than deficiency correction after the fact. (ASSUMPTION: standard construction QC practice; no specific procedural text in accessible sources.)

**Minimum quality baseline (Pass 3 — F-003):** The irreducible quality standard for this project is conformance to IFC specifications and applicable Alberta Safety Codes. This baseline is currently expressed as "final work meets specification requirements" (Specification REQ-QC-06) and "compliance with all applicable Alberta Safety Codes" (REQ-QC-10). The specific minimum quality threshold for each material/system type will be defined once IFC specifications are available. Until then, the prevention principle should be applied against the most restrictive known standard for each trade. (Source: Specification REQ-QC-06, REQ-QC-10; RFP §3.3.2)

### P2 — County visibility is a requirement, not a courtesy

The RFP imposes a hard requirement that the County project representative be present for all inspections and receive copies of all inspection reports (RFP §3.3.2). The QC program must be scheduled and communicated in a manner that makes this attendance achievable. Inspections should not be initiated without adequate County notification lead time (specific lead time TBD — not stated in accessible sources; Procedure should define a minimum lead time or require the QC Plan to establish one based on County PM input).

### P3 — Subcontractors are within QC scope

As Prime Contractor, the Proponent holds QC responsibility for all work performed on site, including work by subcontractors. QC oversight must extend to subcontractor work products with the same inspection and sign-off rigor as direct Proponent work (Specification REQ-QC-07). Subcontractor QC performance feeds directly into the performance evaluation managed under DEL-019-03 (Subcontractor Management).
(Source: RFP §2.15; _DEPENDENCIES.md; decomposition PKG-019 description; Specification REQ-QC-07)

### P4 — Deficiency documentation closes loops

Identifying deficiencies without tracking their remediation to verified closure creates liability exposure under RFP §2.10 (Guarantee Period) and §2.11 (Deficiency Holdback). Every deficiency identified must be logged, classified by severity, assigned, remediated, and verified before the file is closed. The Deficiency Holdback mechanism (Owner retains payment equal to estimated deficiency value until corrected) creates a financial incentive to resolve deficiencies promptly. A severity classification scheme (critical/major/minor) with differentiated response timelines ensures prioritized response to the most impactful deficiencies.
(Source: RFP §2.10, §2.11; Specification REQ-QC-06)

### P5 — QC feeds the weekly meeting cadence

The weekly coordination meeting structure (DEL-019-02) is the primary vehicle for QC status reporting to the County project manager. The QC program should generate standing agenda material (open deficiencies by severity category, upcoming inspections, test results, KPI performance) for this forum rather than creating parallel reporting structures.
(Source: _DEPENDENCIES.md; decomposition DEL-019-02 description; Specification REQ-QC-15)

### P6 — IFC drawings are the conformance baseline

All inspections must reference the current revision of Issued for Construction drawings. Any deviation from IFC drawings that is identified during QC inspection must be documented as a non-conformance and resolved before work progresses. QC records should reference the IFC drawing number and revision used.
(Source: RFP §3.3.2)

---

## Considerations

### Sequencing with upstream deliverables

The QC authority structure flows from DEL-019-01 (Prime Contractor Designation & OH&S Program). The QC Plan should be internally consistent with the OH&S Program — roles, responsibilities, and escalation paths should align across both documents. The QC Manager assignment is declared as an outstanding item in _STATUS.md; this assignment must be resolved before the QC Plan can be finalised.

### Construction phase alignment

The inspection schedule must be phased to match the construction sequence. Given the project involves foundation work (variable-price scope, post-geotech), structural framing, mechanical/electrical/plumbing rough-ins, and building envelope, inspection hold points should be defined for each trade phase. Phase-specific inspection triggers (e.g., pre-pour concrete inspections, pre-close electrical rough-in) are industry standard but not enumerated in accessible sources — QC Plan author to define.
(ASSUMPTION: typical design-build construction phase structure; decomposition PKG-019 context)

### Alberta Safety Codes interface

The RFP requires all work to comply with Alberta Safety Codes (RFP §3.3.2). Safety Code inspections are coordinated through PKG-009 (Permitting & Inspections, DEL-009-04). The QC program should interface with PKG-009 inspection coordination to avoid duplicating requests and to ensure QC hold points align with mandatory Safety Code inspection points. The specific relationship between internal QC inspections and third-party Safety Code inspections is TBD — not explicitly defined in accessible sources.

### Guarantee period forward-linkage

The two-year guarantee period (RFP §2.10) means QC records created during construction will be referenced if defects are identified post-CCC. QC documentation should be retained and filed in a retrievable format for at least the duration of the guarantee period. Specific retention period and format are TBD (see Specification REQ-QC-17 for third-party lab records; Procedure C4 for general record retention requirements).

### Design-Build quality accountability

Under a CCDC 14-2013 Design-Build contract, the Proponent is responsible for both design and construction quality. The QC program must therefore encompass design-to-construction conformance (i.e., ensuring that construction matches the IFC drawings produced under the design packages) as well as workmanship quality. This is a broader scope than a traditional general contractor QC program.
(ASSUMPTION: standard CCDC 14 design-build accountability interpretation; specific CCDC 14-2013 clause text location TBD — see warranted item E-001)

### QC dispute resolution and escalation (Pass 3 — D-002)

When QC findings are contested by subcontractors or when there is disagreement about whether a deficiency has been adequately remediated, a clear escalation pathway is needed to avoid delays in deficiency closure and CCC issuance. The following escalation framework is recommended:

1. **First-level resolution:** QC Manager and subcontractor/trade foreman attempt to resolve on-site.
2. **Second-level escalation:** Project Manager convenes a review with supporting documentation (inspection reports, IFC drawings, test results).
3. **Third-level escalation:** If unresolved, the matter is elevated to the weekly coordination meeting (DEL-019-02) for County PM awareness and input.
4. **Final authority:** TBD — whether the County PM, the Owner, or the Proponent's Project Manager has final authority on disputed QC verdicts is not stated in accessible sources. The QC Plan should define the final authority.

(Source: ASSUMPTION: standard escalation practice for construction QC disputes; no specific dispute resolution procedure in accessible sources. The RFP does not address QC dispute resolution directly.)

### QC program onboarding and training (Pass 3 — E-002)

New inspection personnel, subcontractors, or project team members should be oriented on QC procedures and expectations before they begin work that falls under QC oversight. The QC Plan should address:

- How QC procedures are communicated to new subcontractors during onboarding.
- What orientation or training the QC Manager provides to inspection delegates.
- How changes to QC procedures during construction are communicated to all relevant parties.

(Source: ASSUMPTION: standard construction QC practice requires knowledge transfer mechanisms; no specific onboarding requirement in accessible sources. Procedure should include an onboarding step — see warranted item E-002.)

### Standardized inspection report format (Pass 3 — C-002)

Methodical uniformity across inspectors requires a consistent inspection report format. The QC Plan should include or reference a standard inspection report template that all inspectors (QC Manager, delegates) use for every inspection event. Without a standard template, different inspectors may produce incompatible records that are difficult to aggregate for weekly reporting and CCC documentation. The template should include, at minimum: date, location, inspection type, IFC drawing reference, inspector name, County representative attendance confirmation, findings, deficiencies identified, and sign-off.

(Source: Procedure Phase B, Step B3 references "inspection report form"; Specification Documentation item 3 references "Inspection Reports"; no standard form template exists in accessible sources.)

---

## Trade-offs

| Trade-off | Option A | Option B | Recommended Orientation | Basis |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Inspection frequency | High-frequency continuous oversight (higher cost, earlier deficiency detection) | Milestone-only hold-point inspections (lower cost, higher risk of embedded defects) | Hold-point structure aligned with construction phase milestones, with continuous oversight for high-risk or complex elements | RFP §4.7.5 preference for "detailed and diligent" QC; ASSUMPTION: proportionate approach |
| County notification lead time | Short notice (more flexible scheduling) | Longer notice (ensures County attendance compliance) | Sufficient notice to guarantee County attendance — minimum lead time to be defined in QC Plan based on County PM input (PROPOSAL: 48 hours minimum as default) | REQ-QC-03; RFP §3.3.2 (County attendance is mandatory, not optional); warranted item X-001 |
| Deficiency tracking tool | Spreadsheet-based log | Project management software | Either is acceptable if it produces a traceable, reportable deficiency register with severity classification capability; format TBD | No format requirement in accessible sources |
| QC Plan scope | QC only | Integrated QC + H&S program | Separate documents (QC Plan under DEL-019-04; OH&S Program under DEL-019-01) to maintain clarity of roles and records | _CONTEXT.md; _DEPENDENCIES.md — separate deliverable structure |
| Deficiency severity approach | Treat all deficiencies equally | Classify by severity (critical/major/minor) with differentiated response | Severity classification with differentiated response timelines — enables prioritized resource allocation and risk-proportionate escalation | REQ-QC-06; warranted item E-003 |
| Third-party testing | All testing in-house by QC Manager | Outsource specialized testing to accredited labs | Outsource to accredited labs (ISO 17025 or equivalent) for specialized tests; retain in-house for routine visual inspections | REQ-QC-17; ASSUMPTION: standard practice for specialized materials testing |

---

## Examples

TBD — No project-specific examples of QC Plan structure, inspection report format, or deficiency log format are available in accessible sources. The QC Plan author should consult the Proponent's existing QC program templates and adapt them to project-specific requirements.

| CONF-001 | The RFP §3.1 states the Proponent shall "coordinate inspections" through the County project manager, while §3.3.2 states the Proponent shall "submit all inspection requests" — the coordination chain (direct to Safety Codes authority vs. via County PM) is ambiguous | RFP §3.1 (coordination through County PM) | RFP §3.3.2 (Proponent submits inspection requests) | REQ-QC-03, REQ-QC-04; Procedure Steps B1-B2 | PROPOSAL: Proponent submits all inspection requests; County PM is notified of all requests and receives all reports, consistent with §3.3.2 being a more specific statement (reinforced by warranted item X-004) | TBD |
| CONF-002 | QC Plan approval authority is undefined — not clear whether approval is internal (Project Manager), external (County), or both | _CONTEXT.md — Success Criteria ("documented and approved") | RFP §4.7.5 (no approval authority stated) | REQ-QC-01, REQ-QC-13; Procedure Step A3 | PROPOSAL: Define approval chain in QC Plan; recommend internal approval with County acknowledgement as minimum | TBD |
